summary: The conjunctive form connects adjectives and nouns to a following clause the way English 'and' or 'because' does. い-adjectives drop い and add くて (寒い → 寒くて); な-adjectives and nouns add で (静か → 静かで; 学生 → 学生で). The exception is いい, which becomes よくて.
subUses: Linking qualities in sequence: 空が青くて海もきれいだった (The sky was blue and the sea was beautiful too)
Expressing cause or reason: 試験が難しくて困った (I was troubled because the exam was difficult)
Linking noun predicates: 今日は休みで、明日も休みだ (Today is a day off, and tomorrow is too)
cautions: The て/で form can be read as neutral 'and' or causal 'because' — context determines which fits; do not force one interpretation
Exception: いい → よくて (not いくて); mark いくて wrong
Noun + で here is the て-form of the copula だ (だ → で), not the location or means particle で — judge by whether a noun is being linked to another predicate

summary: The causative form expresses making or letting someone do something. For godan verbs, change the final vowel to the あ-row and add せる (話す→話させる); for ichidan verbs, drop る and add させる (食べる→食べさせる); irregulars: する→させる, くる→こさせる.
subUses: Forcing/compelling: 先生は学生を立たせた (The teacher made the student stand up)
Permitting/allowing: 母は子供にケーキを食べさせた (The mother let the child eat cake)
Causative + てあげる/てくれる (strong 'let' reading): 好きな本を読ませてくれた (They let me read the book I wanted)
Causative of intransitive verbs for indirect causation: 木に実をならせる (to make a tree bear fruit)
cautions: Particle choice for the causee depends primarily on verb transitivity: intransitive verbs use を for the causee (学生を立たせた); transitive verbs use に to avoid double を (子供にケーキを食べさせた). The に=letting/を=forcing distinction taught in textbooks is a secondary tendency, not the primary rule
When てあげる/てくれる/てもらう follows the causative, it almost always means 'let' rather than 'make'
Do not confuse causative させる with passive される — they look similar for する but differ for other verbs
The short causative (す instead of せる, e.g. 行かす instead of 行かせる) is colloquial — accept it but note it is informal

summary: い-adjectives become adverbs by replacing the final い with く (早い→早く, 大きい→大きく). な-adjectives become adverbs by adding に (静か→静かに). These adverbs modify verbs to describe how an action is performed, equivalent to English '-ly' adverbs.
subUses: Manner adverbs from い-adjectives: 早く走る (run quickly)
Manner adverbs from な-adjectives: 丁寧に書く (write carefully)
Adverbial use with なる for change of state: 大きくなる (become big)
Standalone adverbs that are neither い nor な derived: いっぱい食べる (eat a lot) — these function adverbially without a particle
cautions: いい becomes よく (not いく) — this is the only irregular い-adjective adverb
Some words like いっぱい function as adverbs without any conjugation or particle — they are 'true adverbs' rather than adjective-derived adverbs
The く form of い-adjectives also serves as the conjunctive/continuative form (美しく、優しい人 = a beautiful and kind person) — don't confuse this linking use with the adverbial use

summary: Two contrastive patterns express the speaker's attitude toward a quantity. Number + も (with an affirmative predicate) conveys surprise or emphasis that the number is large: 七台も持っている means 'has as many as seven.' Number + しか (with a negative predicate) conveys that the number is smaller than expected: 一冊しか読まなかった means 'read only one.' The choice reflects the speaker's subjective evaluation, not an objective threshold.
subUses: Emphasizing a large quantity with も: メアリーさんはアルバイトが三つもあります (Mary has as many as three part-time jobs — the speaker finds this a lot)
Emphasizing a small quantity with しか～ない: けんさんは友だちが一人しかいません (Ken has only one friend — the speaker finds this few)
cautions: The choice between も and しか is always the speaker's subjective judgment, not an objective rule. The same number can take either particle depending on context: 三本も (as many as three, if three seems like a lot to the speaker) vs 三本しか (only three, if three seems like few).
しか requires a negative predicate (ない, ません, etc.). Using しか with an affirmative predicate is ungrammatical.
も in this pattern is different from the additive も meaning 'also/too'. Context (appearing after a counter word) disambiguates.
Vary the domain across questions: use physical objects with counter words (本, 匹, 台, 冊), time durations (時間, 分), and amounts of money (円) — do not default to the same type repeatedly.

summary: Japanese has paired verbs where one is transitive (他動詞, takes a direct object with を) and the other is intransitive (自動詞, the subject undergoes the change with が). For example, 開ける (to open something) vs 開く (something opens). The transitive verb implies a deliberate agent acting on an object; the intransitive verb describes a change happening on its own or focuses on the result.
subUses: Deliberate action with transitive verb: ドアを閉めた (I closed the door)
Spontaneous change with intransitive verb: ドアが閉まった (The door closed)
Describing resulting states with intransitive て-form + いる: 電気がついている (The light is on)
Expressing accidental or uncontrolled events: コップが割れた (The cup broke — no agent implied)
cautions: Particle choice is the key signal: transitive uses を for the object; intransitive uses が for the subject undergoing change
Some verbs look similar but have different transitivity — つく (intransitive, to be attached) vs つける (transitive, to attach)
する and なる can be thought of as a transitive/intransitive pair: Xにする (make it X) vs Xになる (become X)
Not all verbs have a transitive/intransitive partner — some exist only as one type (e.g., つまずく is intransitive with no transitive counterpart; ある is intransitive with no direct transitive pair)

summary: In Japanese, verbs and adjectives modify nouns by directly preceding them in plain (short) form — no relative pronoun like 'that' or 'which' is needed. The modifying clause can use any plain-form tense: dictionary form for habitual or future actions, た-form for completed or past actions, and ている-form for ongoing or resultant states. Adjectives follow the same pattern: い-adjectives precede the noun unchanged, while な-adjectives drop だ and take な. When the embedded clause has an explicit subject, it is marked with が, not は.
subUses: Non-past verb (habitual/future): 毎朝走る人 (a person who runs every morning)
Past-action (た-form): 昨日読んだ本 (the book I read yesterday)
Ongoing state (ている-form): 眠っている子供 (a sleeping child / a child who is sleeping)
Potential verb: 泳げる子供 (a child who can swim)
い-adjective: 目が青い人 (a person with blue eyes)
な-adjective: 静かな場所 (a quiet place)
Gap noun as object of the embedded verb: 妹が作ったケーキ (the cake my sister made — ケーキ is the object of 作った)
Gap noun as indirect object or recipient: 友達にもらった本 (the book I received from a friend — 本 is the received item)
cautions: Never use ます-form in a relative clause: 住んでいる人 is correct; 住んでいます人 is ungrammatical
な-adjectives drop だ and add な directly before the noun: 元気な子 not 元気だ子
The noun being modified can fill any argument role in the embedded clause — subject, object, location, recipient, etc. The position is implied; no gap marker is used
The embedded clause subject takes が, not は: 犬が好きな人 (a person who likes dogs) — は cannot replace が here

summary: The passive form is made by changing godan verbs to the あ-row + れる (書く→書かれる) and ichidan verbs by replacing る with られる (食べる→食べられる); irregulars: する→される, くる→こられる. The agent is marked with に. Japanese passive has both a direct passive (equivalent to English passive) and an indirect or 'suffering' passive where the subject is adversely affected by someone else's action.
subUses: Adverse affective (intransitive): 雨に降られた (I got rained on — adversely affected by someone else's action even though I wasn't involved)
Adverse affective (possessive): 友達に日記を読まれた (My friend read my diary on me — adversely affected through possession of the object)
Neutral affective: 先輩にパーティーに誘われた (I was invited to a party by my senpai — not adverse, just a thing that happened to me)
Direct passive (non-human subject, no agent): ここに公園が作られた (A park was built here — written-language style, inanimate subject, no agent mentioned)
Direct passive (non-human subject, named human agent with によって): この絵はピカソによって描かれた (This picture was painted by Picasso — によって marks the agent in direct passive, never in affective passive)
Honorific: 先生が話された (The teacher spoke — respectful/polite register)
cautions: For ichidan verbs, passive られる and potential られる are identical in form — context distinguishes them
ら抜き言葉 (dropping ら) applies ONLY to the potential meaning, never to the passive — 食べれる is colloquial potential, but the passive is always 食べられる
The adverse affective (intransitive) passive has no English equivalent — the subject is harmed by an event even though they have no grammatical role in the underlying action

に marks the agent in affective passive (human or rain etc.); によって marks the agent only in direct passive with a named human agent — do not use に+human-agent in direct passive or によって+non-human in affective passive
から can replace に for the agent when there is no physical contact: みんなから愛されている (loved by everyone)

summary: あげる, くれる, and もらう are the three basic Japanese giving/receiving verbs. あげる means the giver gives outward (away from the speaker's sphere); くれる means someone gives inward toward the speaker or the speaker's in-group; もらう reverses the perspective so the recipient is the subject (X は Y に/から もらう). The giver is marked with は/が and the recipient with に for both あげる and くれる.
subUses: Giving outward (あげる): 私はスーさんに辞書をあげました (I gave Sue a dictionary)
Third-party giving (あげる): けんさんはスーさんに猫をあげました (Ken gave Sue a cat)
Giving directly to the speaker (くれる): スーさんは私にTシャツをくれました (Sue gave me a T-shirt)

Giving from an outsider to someone in the speaker's in-group (くれる): 大統領が妹に手紙をくれました (The president gave my sister a letter — くれる because the speaker identifies with their sister, and the giver is distant)
Receiving with もらう (recipient as subject): 私はけんさんに帽子をもらいました (I received a hat from Ken)
Asking what was received: 誕生日に何をもらいましたか (What did you get for your birthday?)
cautions: あげる and くれる cannot be swapped freely — use あげる when the gift moves away from the speaker; use くれる only when the recipient is the speaker or someone the speaker feels close to
もらう makes the recipient the grammatical subject; the giver takes に or から (私は友だちに/から本をもらった)
Do not use くれる when speaking of something given to a third party outside the speaker's in-group — use あげる instead
These base giving verbs (for objects) are distinct from the て-form giving verbs (てあげる/てくれる/てもらう) which express doing favors; do not confuse the two patterns

summary: Verb stem (ます-stem) + かねる expresses that the speaker finds it difficult, emotionally hesitant, or impossible to perform an action. It implies a degree of inner willingness thwarted by circumstances, making it a softer, more polite alternative to できない. Frequently used in formal and business contexts where blunt refusal would be inappropriate.
subUses: Polite formal refusal: そのご要望にはお応えしかねます (I'm afraid we cannot accommodate that request)
Genuine difficulty or reluctant inability: その判断には賛成しかねる (I find myself unable to agree with that judgment)
Classical/literary usage: 流れる涙止めそかねつる (the flowing tears I could not stop) — classical auxiliary forms may appear in older texts and songs
cautions: Primarily attaches to transitive verbs; わかる and できる are accepted exceptions — avoid attaching かねる to arbitrary intransitive verbs
Do not confuse with かねない, which means 'might well / quite possible that' — かねない expresses likelihood, not inability
Register is formal/written; in casual speech できない or しにくい is more natural — かねる sounds stiff in everyday conversation

summary: かもしれない (polite: かもしれません) expresses that something is possible but uncertain — roughly 'might' or 'maybe'. It attaches directly to the plain (short) form of verbs and い-adjectives; な-adjectives and nouns drop the copula だ before it (元気かもしれない, not 元気だかもしれない). The colloquial abbreviation かも appears frequently at the end of casual sentences.
subUses: Future uncertainty with verbs: 雨が降るかもしれない (It might rain)
Speculation about current states with adjectives: さむいかもしれません (It might be cold)
Conjecture about people or things with nouns: 今日はスーさんの誕生日かもしれません (Today might be Sue's birthday)
Speculation about past events using past plain form: プロポーズしたかもしれません (He might have proposed)
Negative speculation: 友だちがいないかもしれません (She might not have any friends)
cautions: な-adjectives and nouns do NOT take だ before かもしれない — 元気かもしれない is correct; 元気だかもしれない is ungrammatical
かもしれない expresses lower certainty than でしょう/だろう; use かもしれない when genuinely unsure, でしょう when you expect agreement or higher probability
The shortened form かも is very common in casual speech and should be accepted as correct; かもしれません is the polite equivalent

summary: Verb[dictionary]+ことにする means 'decide to do X' — a volitional choice made by the subject. The negative form Verb[ない]+ことにする means 'decide not to do X.' The ongoing form ことにしている extends this decision into a standing rule or habit: 'make it a point to do X' or 'have a policy of doing X.'
subUses: One-time decision (affirmative): 留学することにしました (Decided to study abroad)
One-time decision (negative): オーストラリアに帰らないことにしました (Decided not to return to Australia)
Standing self-imposed rule (ことにしている, affirmative): 毎日新聞を読むことにしています (I make it a rule to read the newspaper every day)
Standing self-imposed rule (ことにしている, negative): テレビを見ながら勉強しないことにしています (I have a rule not to study while watching TV)
cautions: All sentences for this topic should reflect the subject's own volition — ことにする expresses a deliberate personal choice
ことにしている describes a self-imposed ongoing rule, not a fresh one-time decision — it implies the habit has already been established and continues
ことにしている expresses a firm, already-decided rule — not tentative ongoing effort toward a goal
The verb before こと must be plain dictionary form (affirmative) or ない-form (negative) — not て-form or ます-form

summary: The particle し lists multiple reasons or properties of equal weight, attached to short-form predicates (verb/い-adj as-is, な-adj+だ, noun+だ). It functions like 'and besides' or 'and also,' presenting co-existing reasons that together support a conclusion.
subUses: Listing multiple reasons: 安いし、おいしいし、あの店が好きだ (It's cheap and tasty — that's why I like that restaurant)
Single reason with implied others: 時間もないし、帰ろう (We're out of time [among other reasons], so let's head back)
Listing qualities without an explicit conclusion: あの先生は優しいし、説明も上手だし (That teacher is kind and good at explaining...)
cautions: し requires short-form predicates — だ appears after な-adjectives and nouns (静かだし) but NOT after い-adjectives (高いし, not 高いだし)
All reasons listed with し should carry roughly equal weight — if one reason clearly dominates, から or ので is more natural
し can appear just once (with other reasons implied) — the doubled し〜し pattern is common but not required
Do not confuse with the conjunctive particle て for sequential actions — し lists co-existing reasons without implying temporal order

summary: The suffix そう expresses a visual or sensory impression — 'looks like,' 'seems like,' or 'appears to be.' Attach it to the stem of い-adjectives (drop い: 高い→高そう), directly to な-adjective stems (元気→元気そう), and to verb stems (降る→降りそう). そうな/そうに modify nouns and verbs respectively.
subUses: Visual impression from い-adjective: この指輪は高そうです (This ring looks expensive)
Visual impression from な-adjective: 元気そうな子供ですね (That child looks healthy)
Prediction about an upcoming event from verb stem: 雨が降りそうです (It looks like it's going to rain)
Attributive modifier with そうな: 面白そうなDVDですね (That DVD looks interesting)
cautions: Exception: いい becomes よさそう (not いそう) — さ is inserted before そう
Negative forms: ない always becomes なさそう (さ inserted): 食べなさそう (doesn't look like they'll eat), 高くなさそう (doesn't look expensive). Do not produce なそう

Do NOT confuse with hearsay そうだ ('I heard that'): both use そうだ but attach differently — 'looks like' attaches to the adjective/verb stem (い dropped, verb in stem form), while hearsay attaches to the plain/dictionary form. When generating sentences for this topic, only produce stem-attached そう forms
そう vs らしい: そう expresses a guess based on direct visual/sensory evidence; らしい expresses inference based on information or secondhand evidence

summary: Verb[た]+ことがある expresses past experience — 'have done X before' or 'have experienced X at least once.' The こと nominalizes the verb phrase, and ある/ない confirms whether the experience exists in one's history.
subUses: Past experience (affirmative): 日本に行ったことがあります (I have been to Japan before)
Past experience (negative / never): 天ぷらを食べたことがありません (I have never eaten tempura)
Experience with distant past time adverb: 五年前に日本へ行ったことがある (I went to Japan five years ago — simultaneously asserts the experience and when it occurred)
cautions: The pattern requires Verb[た]+こと+が+ある/ない — こと is fixed here and cannot be replaced by の
Always use Verb[た]+ことがある — this is because dictionary-form ことがある (without た) is a separate grammar point and should not appear in sentences for this topic
たことがある describes cumulative experience up to the present — the ある/ない at the end stays in present form even when a past time adverb is present (五年前に行ったことがある). Do not change ある to あった
When using a past time adverb with this pattern, the time must be clearly distant from the present. Very recent adverbs (きのう, さっき, etc.) are unnatural — do not combine them with たことがある

summary: たら is formed by adding ら to the short-form past: verb (行った → 行ったら), い-adjective (安い → 安かったら), な-adjective (静かだ → 静かだったら), noun (休みだ → 休みだったら). It expresses 'if/when A, then B,' focusing on the completion or occurrence of A as the trigger for B.
subUses: Hypothetical condition: お金があったら、旅行したい (If I had money, I'd want to travel)
Sequential or temporal 'when': 家に帰ったら、電話して (Call me when you get home)
Past discovery — something found upon doing A: 窓を開けたら、雨が降っていた (When I opened the window, it was raining)
cautions: たら is natural with commands and requests in the B clause: 時間があったら来てください is correct
For timeless or habitual conditions, ば or と is more natural than たら; たら works best for one-time or specific events
Do not confuse with たらどう, which expresses a suggestion ('Why don't you...?'); たらどう is a separate construction

In English, 'when' can introduce uncertain future conditionals just as 'if' can (e.g. 'When it rains, the sports day will be canceled' is a natural English sentence with no strong implication of certainty). This makes 'if' vs. 'when' a poor distractor pair — a native English speaker could accept either as a translation of たら. Instead, distractors should reflect grammar points with clearly distinct Japanese meanings, such as 〜と (inevitable/habitual consequence), 〜ば (general condition), or 〜なら (topic-based reasoning), and the English choices should reflect those distinct meanings rather than near-synonymous English conditionals.

summary: たらどうですか (casual: たらどう) offers advice or a suggestion to someone who has expressed a problem — roughly 'Why don't you...?' or 'How about...?'. It is formed from the past tense short form (た-form) of a verb followed by どうですか. The pattern is specifically for advice in response to a stated problem, not for invitations.
subUses: Advice responding to a problem: 疲れているんです → 早く寝たらどうですか (You're tired? Why don't you go to bed early?)
Suggestion to take action: 財布をなくしたんです → 警察に行ったらどうですか (You lost your wallet? Why don't you go to the police?)
Casual shortening: 薬を飲んだらどう？ (Why don't you take some medicine?)
cautions: Use たらどうですか for advice in response to someone's expressed problem, not for invitations — for invitations use ませんか instead
たらどうですか can sound critical depending on tone; たほうがいい is a more direct recommendation, and ませんか is a warmer invitation
The construction uses the た-form (past plain form) of the verb, not the て-form — 行ったらどうですか, not 行ってどうですか
Politeness scale: たらどう (casual) → たらどうですか (polite) → たらどうでしょう(か) / たらいかがですか (more formal). Accept any of these as correct.

summary: たり～たりする lists actions or states as non-exhaustive examples, equivalent to "do things like A and B." Form it by adding り to the plain past (た-form) of each verb, then close the chain with する (conjugated for tense and politeness). Unlike the て-form sequence, たり～たりする carries no implied order among the listed items.
subUses: 列挙 (listing representative actions): 音楽を聞いたり、本を読んだりした — implies other activities also occurred.
交互 (alternating or fluctuating states): 雨が降ったり止んだりしている — it keeps going back and forth between raining and stopping.
単独 (single-verb vagueness): 散歩したりする — one verb + たり + する implies other unspecified activities exist.
cautions: て-form vs. たり: て-form (食べて寝た) sequences events in strict order and treats the list as complete; たり～たりする (食べたり寝たりした) presents events as examples with no order and implies the list is open-ended.
The list is always non-exhaustive: using たり～たりする implies "among other things," so it sounds odd when the speaker intends to list every action precisely. Prefer て-form for exhaustive sequences.
たりして at sentence end: this fixed phrase (plain-past verb + たりして) expresses joking speculation or self-deprecating hedging (e.g. 間違えたりして). It is a distinct usage, not a regular たり list.
Conjugation: the final する carries all tense and politeness marking. The たり particles themselves do not change.

summary: Attaching おく to the て-form of a verb describes doing something ahead of time so it is ready or in place for a future event. Formation: verb て-form + おく (食べておく, 読んでおく). In casual speech, ておく is regularly contracted to とく (読んどく, 買っとく).
subUses: Preparatory action — doing something now so it is ready later: 旅行の前にホテルを予約しておいた (reserved a hotel in advance of the trip). This includes preventative actions where the future event is something undesirable: 虫歯にならないように歯を磨いておく (brushing teeth in advance so cavities don't develop).
Maintaining a state intentionally — leaving something as it is on purpose: 窓を開けておいた (I left the window open [for a reason]); このままにしておいてください (please leave it as it is).
Causative + ておく — letting someone or something remain in its current state: 疲れているからしばらく寝させておこう (they're tired so let's leave them sleeping for a while).

Deliberate inaction (ないでおく) — intentionally leaving something undone: フレッドには言わないでおきましょう (Let's leave it unsaid to Fred / Let's not tell Fred). The negative て-form ないで + おく frames the non-action as a conscious, purposeful choice rather than mere omission.

Tentative or stopgap action — doing something for now even if it is not the ideal solution, often paired with とりあえず: とりあえず、なんか頼んでおく？ (Should we order something to get started [even though not everyone is here yet]?); このトピックはこの辺で終わりにしておきましょう (Let's wrap up this topic here for now).
Casual contraction とく: 後で読むから、ここに置いといて (I'll read it later, so leave it here)
cautions: ておく vs てある: ておく emphasizes preparatory intent (doing the action on purpose for later); てある describes only the resulting state (the thing is in a done state). They are not interchangeable.
The casual contractions とく/どく (from ておく/でおく) are standard spoken Japanese — do not mark them wrong.

Causative + ておく can be ambiguous between 'let someone do X for future convenience' and simply 'let someone do X.' For example, 私はボブにビールを飲ませておいた can mean either (A) I let Bob drink beer (for some future purpose) or (B) I simply let Bob drink beer. Context is needed to resolve this.

summary: Attach しまう to the て-form of a verb to express that an action was carried out completely or resulted in an unfortunate, irreversible outcome. Both senses share the core idea that the action is fully completed and cannot be undone; the difference is only whether that finality is unwelcome or simply neutral. Colloquial contractions: て + しまう → ちゃう; で + しまう → じゃう (e.g., 飲んでしまう → 飲んじゃう).
subUses: Thorough completion: 宿題をやってしまった (I got all the homework done — finished it completely, no going back)
Regrettable or accidental outcome (also fully completed): 財布を忘れてしまった (I went and forgot my wallet — it's done, can't go back)
cautions: Context determines whether the meaning is completion or regret — the same form could carry both simultaneously so do not force exactly one reading
The completion sense is often reinforced by adverbs like すっかり (completely) or 全部 (all); their presence is a strong signal that completion, not regret, is the primary reading
Colloquial contractions ちゃう and じゃう are gender-neutral in casual speech; do not mark them wrong in informal contexts
Additional male-only contractions: ちまう and じまう follow the same て/で rule (e.g., 読んでしまう → 読んじまう); these are correct but gender-restricted

summary: てならない expresses an uncontrollable, persistent feeling or sensation — 'can't help but feel' or 'extremely.' It attaches to the て-form of verbs and い-adjectives, or で for な-adjectives (さびしくてならない, 気になってならない). It is a formal, literary expression more common in writing than speech.
subUses: Uncontrollable emotion: 残念でならない (I can't help but feel regret)
Persistent sensation: 暑くてならない (It's unbearably hot)
Spontaneous feeling: 気がしてならない (I can't shake the feeling that...)
cautions: The feeling must be ongoing and persistent, not momentary — てならない is wrong for sudden, fleeting reactions
More formal than てたまらない (which stresses unbearable intensity) and てしょうがない (which is conversational)
In polite speech, なりません replaces ならない: さびしくてなりません
Only used for spontaneous, involuntary feelings — not for deliberate actions or controllable states

summary: ては〜ては expresses repeated alternation between two actions, often implying a cycle or habitual pattern. The structure is Verb-てはVerb-ては, where the two verbs are typically contrasting or complementary actions. It can also appear with a single ては when context already implies repetition.
subUses: Repeated alternation between two actions: 食べては寝、食べては寝の生活だ (A life of eating then sleeping, eating then sleeping)
Habitual cycle leading to a result: 練習しては失敗し、また練習した (Practiced and failed, practiced and failed again)
Single ては with implied repetition: たんこぶをつくっては渡した (kept making bumps and handing them out — repeated giving)
cautions: ては can appear only once when the sentence structure already conveys repetition — the doubled form is not always required
Contracted forms ちゃ and じゃ are acceptable in casual speech, but must be used consistently (don't mix ては and ちゃ in the same sentence)

The same ては form also appears in conditional/consequential patterns like てはいけない (must not) and ては困る (it would be a problem if). These share the literal structure 'if you do X' but are fixed expressions, not the repetitive-alternation pattern — distinguish by whether the sentence describes a recurring cycle or a one-time consequence

summary: てみる attaches to the て-form of a verb and means 'do something and see how it goes' — trying an action to observe the outcome. It is often translated as 'try doing X' when the speaker has not done it before or is testing a possibility. てみる conjugates like a regular ichidan verb (てみます, てみた, てみたい).
subUses: First-time or exploratory attempt: 空手のクラスに行ってみます (I'll try going to a karate class [to see what it's like])
Acting on a suggestion: じゃあ、食べてみます (Well then, I'll try eating it)
Expressing desire to experience something new: モンゴルに行ってみたいです (I'd like to try going to Mongolia)
Checking a result by doing: 図書館で調べてみます (I'll try looking it up at the library [and see what I find])
cautions: てみる focuses on the action and its observed result, not on whether success is expected. Compare with Verb[volitional]+とする, which implies attempting something that may not succeed
Past tense てみた carries an implication that the attempt was made and completed; it does not inherently signal failure or success — context determines the outcome
Do not confuse with てごらん, which is a directive form ('give it a try') used toward someone of lower status or children; てみる applies equally to self and others
In very casual speech, the final る may be dropped (食べてみ) — accept this but note it is informal

summary: など is a particle placed after a noun (or a list of nouns) to indicate that the mentioned items are examples from a larger, unstated set. It translates as 'such as,' 'things like,' or 'etc.' It can also precede の to modify a following noun: Nounなど+の+Noun.
subUses: Listing with open-ended implication: コーヒーや紅茶などを飲んだ (I drank things like coffee and tea)
Modifying a following noun with などの: 本などの荷物を棚に置いた (I put luggage such as books on the shelf)
Self-deprecating or humble use (with negated predicate): 私などにはとても無理だ (It's quite impossible for someone like me)
cautions: など implies the list is not exhaustive — do not use it when the enumeration is meant to be complete
With a negated predicate, など conveys humbleness (when the subject is the speaker or their in-group) or derogation (otherwise); なんか intensifies the derogatory nuance
なんか is the informal colloquial version of など and can be used the same way, but in derogatory contexts it carries stronger emphasis; neither なんか nor なんて is appropriate in formal writing
The kanji form 等 carries the same meaning and is common in written Japanese; both forms are correct

summary: なら (short for ならば) is a conditional particle meaning 'if it is the case that X' or 'as for X.' Its defining feature is contrast and limitation: it picks out one item or situation and applies the predicate specifically to that, implying different treatment for other items. It attaches directly to nouns, な-adjective stems, plain-form verbs, and い-adjectives, optionally with の before it for nominalization.
subUses: Limiting/contrastive topic with noun: 魚なら食べますが、肉は食べません (Fish I'll eat, but not meat)
Contrastive availability: テニスならできます (Tennis I can do [implying other things I cannot])
Responding to a condition raised by the other speaker: 宿題が難しいなら、一緒にしよう (If the homework is hard, let's do it together)
Hypothetical about a specific person or thing: 君ならできる (If it's you, you can do it — implying not everyone could)
cautions: なら's primary meaning is contrastive limitation ('as for X specifically') — this is why it frequently appears with が or は contrasts in the second clause (魚なら食べますが、肉は食べません). This distinguishes it from たら and ば, which are simple conditionals without the contrastive nuance
When responding to what a conversation partner just said, なら picks up that topic and comments on it: Aが高いなら、Bにしよう. This 'topic-responsive' use is natural and common, especially in speech

Attachment: なら follows nouns and な-adjective stems directly (日曜日なら, 元気なら); for verb and い-adjective phrases, both plain form + なら and plain form + のなら are used — adding の makes the nominalized clause more explicit
Do not confuse なら with the homophonous place name 奈良 (Nara, Japan) in quiz sentences

summary: The particle に marks a recurring time period within which an action happens a set number of times — equivalent to English 'per' or 'every.' The structure is: (time period) に (count/duration) + verb. The period comes before に; the frequency or duration follows it.
subUses: Count per time period: 一週間に一回スーパーに行きます (I go to the supermarket once a week)
Duration per time period: 一日に一時間日本語を勉強します (I study Japanese one hour per day)
Rate per recurring unit: 一時間に十本の電車があります (There are ten trains per hour)
cautions: Do not confuse に (frequency) with で (total time): 三日でこの本を読んだ means 'read the book in 3 days total (start to finish),' whereas 一日に二時間読む means '2 hours per day (recurring).' に marks a slot within a repeating cycle; で marks the total span consumed
The time period always precedes に and the count/duration follows: 一週間に三回 (three times per week) — reversing the order is unnatural
Related patterns: ごとに (every X, marking each unit individually: 二時間ごとに = every two hours) and おきに (at intervals of X: 二時間おきに = every other two hours, i.e. with a gap of two hours between)

summary: なる expresses a change of state: 'to become (A).' い-adjectives attach くなる to the stem (大きい → 大きくなる); な-adjectives and nouns attach になる (静か → 静かになる; 医者 → 医者になる). The exception is いい, which becomes よくなる.
subUses: Change in quality (い-adjective): 外が暗くなった (It got dark outside)
Change in quality (な-adjective): 部屋が静かになった (The room became quiet)
Change in role or category (noun): 医者になりたい (I want to become a doctor)
Gradual ongoing change with てくる: だんだん上手になってきた (I've gradually been getting better)
cautions: Exception: いい → よくなる; mark いくなる wrong
なる describes spontaneous change without an agent; to express making something change, use にする or くする instead
なっている indicates the resulting state persists (e.g., 寒くなっている = it has become cold and remains so), not an ongoing process of becoming

summary: によって / による / により are three surface forms of the same grammatical expression, all built on the verb よる (to depend on, to be due to). によって and により introduce an agent, cause, means, or basis before a comma; による appears before a following noun as a modifier (その火事による被害 — damage due to that fire) or at the end of a sentence. により is a more formal or written variant of によって. The construction covers four overlapping meanings: agent in a passive clause, cause or reason, method or means, and variation depending on circumstances.
subUses: Agent in a passive clause (named human or institution): この論文は田中先生によって書かれた (This paper was written by Professor Tanaka)
Cause or reason: 地震によって多くの建物が倒れた (Many buildings collapsed due to the earthquake)
Method or means: 話し合いによって解決した (Resolved through discussion)
Variation depending on circumstances: 結果は人によって違う (Results differ depending on the person)
Noun modifier with による: 事故による遅延 (delay caused by an accident)
cautions: In a passive clause, によって marks a named human or institutional agent; に marks the agent in the affective/suffering passive — do not swap them
による before a noun is a noun-modifying clause meaning 'caused by' or 'based on'; do not confuse this modifier によって with the location particle に followed by unrelated words
により is a more written/formal substitute for によって in cause and means senses — both should be accepted as correct; により rarely appears in casual speech

summary: ぬ is the attributive form of the classical auxiliary ず, which negates verbs the same way modern ない does. Attach ぬ to the same stem that takes ない (the irrealis / 未然形 stem): 書く → 書かぬ, 食べる → 食べぬ. Irregular forms: する → せぬ, くる → こぬ, いる (via its classical counterpart おる) → おらぬ. The form appears in proverbs, set phrases, songs, literary fiction, and formal or historical writing.
subUses: Classical negation modifying a noun: 帰らぬ人 (a person who does not return)
Negating a compound verbal phrase (classical おる replaces いる): されておらぬ (is not being done)
Set phrases frozen in classical form: 隅に置けぬ人 (a person not to be taken lightly)
cautions: する negates to せぬ, not しぬ — mark しぬ wrong (しぬ means 'to die')
くる negates to こぬ; いる negates via its classical counterpart おる → おらぬ
ぬ is attributive (modifies a following noun); the adverbial/conjunctive classical negative is ず — do not use ぬ as a standalone sentence-ending negative in a formal-modern register

summary: The particle の nominalizes verb phrases, turning them into noun-like units that can fill subject, object, or topic positions in a sentence. Verb+のは makes the verb phrase a topic ('doing X is...'), Verb+のが marks it as a subject (often with adjectives like 好き, 上手), and Verb+のを marks it as a direct object. This is one of the most fundamental Japanese grammar structures.
subUses: Nominalizing as topic with のは: 泳ぐのは楽しい (Swimming is fun)
Nominalizing as subject with のが + emotive adjective: 絵を描くのが好きだ (I like drawing pictures)
Nominalizing as object with のを: 鳥が飛ぶのを見た (I saw a bird fly)
Cleft sentence / exhaustive listing with のは...だ: 早く咲くのは東京だ (The one that blooms earliest is Tokyo)
cautions: の and こと are often interchangeable for nominalization, but こと is required in set phrases: ことができる, ことがある — の cannot substitute here

の followed by だ/です forms the explanatory のだ/んです construction — this is grammatical but has a different function (explanation/emphasis) from plain nominalization. Do not tell students that の + だ is ungrammatical
In the cleft sentence pattern (Xのは Y だ), のは highlights Y as the specific answer — this is a focus/emphasis construction, not simple nominalization

summary: のように and のような express similarity or comparison — 'like (noun)' or 'in the manner of (noun).' のように modifies verbs and adjectives (how something is done), while のような modifies nouns (what kind of thing). Both require の after the comparison noun. This is the more formal equivalent of みたいに/みたいな.
subUses: Simile modifying a verb: 鳥のように飛びたい (I want to fly like a bird)
Simile modifying a noun: 夢のような話 (A story like a dream)
Counterfactual simile ('as if'), often with まるで: まるで別人のように話した (spoke as if they were a completely different person)
Giving an example: このような場合はどうしますか (What do you do in cases like this?)
cautions: のように/のような vs みたいに/みたいな is primarily a register difference: よう is formal/written, みたい is casual/spoken. Some textbooks claim a semantic distinction (direct knowledge vs observation) but this is not reliably borne out in practice

Attachment rules: after a comparison noun, の is required (鳥のように). When the preceding element is a full clause (verb, い-adjective, or な-adjective predicate), よう attaches directly: 走っているように, 高いように, 静かなように (な-adjectives keep their な). Do not insert の between a clause and よう.

topics: bunpro:ようと思う-おうと思う
summary: The plain volitional form expresses a casual invitation or internal decision. Ichidan verbs drop る and add よう (食べる → 食べよう); godan verbs replace the final vowel with the お-row equivalent and add う (行く → 行こう, 話す → 話そう); irregulars: する → しよう, くる → こよう. When combined with と思う or と思っています, the volitional expresses intention rather than invitation.
subUses: Casual suggestion or invitation (informal equivalent of ましょう): カフェでコーヒーを飲もうか (shall we drink coffee at a café?)
Volitional + か to soften a suggestion or ask for agreement: どこで食べようか (where shall we eat?)
Volitional + と思います for a decision made just now: 今日から毎日運動しようと思います (I've just decided to exercise daily starting today)
Volitional + と思っています for an intention formed earlier and still ongoing: 来年日本に留学しようと思っています (I've been planning to study abroad in Japan next year)
cautions: Volitional + と思います vs volitional + と思っています: と思います marks a decision made right now in the conversation; と思っています marks an ongoing intention decided earlier. Depending on when the decision was formed, one or the other applies.
The volitional form + と思います specifically marks *intention* or *decision*. Distinguish this from predictions, which use non-volitional forms (e.g., 雨が降ると思います).
The volitional is the plain/casual register for suggestions and intentions; ましょう is the polite register. Context determines register choice, not grammatical function.

summary: 代わりに means 'instead of,' 'in place of,' or 'to make up for.' The pattern is [A] の代わりに [B], where B substitutes for or compensates for A. A can be a noun phrase (Nounの代わりに) or a verb clause (verb dictionary form + 代わりに). Word order rule: the noun immediately before の代わりに is always the original thing being replaced (the unavailable or less desirable item); the substitute appears after 代わりに, typically as the subject or object of the verb.
subUses: Noun substitution: バターの代わりにマーガリンを使った (I used margarine instead of butter)
Role substitution: 私の代わりに発表してもらえますか (Could you present in my place?)
Compensation for a prior action: 昨日休んだ代わりに今日は遅くまで働いた (I worked late today to make up for resting yesterday)
cautions: 代わりに expresses direct substitution or compensation; for a preferred alternative without a one-for-one replacement, むしろ ('rather') is more appropriate
に代わって is a formal variant used for taking over someone's official role or speaking on their behalf — accept it as correct but note the register difference

Word order is critical: the noun before の代わりに must be the thing being replaced, not the substitute. Always double-check that [replaced thing]の代わりに [substitute] reads correctly. Example: 私のコンピュータの代わりに友人のラップトップを使った — 'my computer' is the replaced item, 'friend's laptop' is the substitute.

summary: The て-form is one of the most versatile verb conjugations in Japanese. For godan verbs, the ending changes based on the final consonant (く→いて, ぐ→いで, す→して, む/ぶ/ぬ→んで, う/つ/る→って); ichidan verbs drop る and add て; irregulars: する→して, くる→きて. The て-form connects clauses and is the basis for many grammatical constructions.
subUses: Sequential actions: 起きて、顔を洗って、朝ごはんを食べた (Got up, washed my face, and ate breakfast)
Requests with ください: 窓を開けてください (Please open the window)
Means/method: バスに乗って学校に行く (Go to school by taking the bus)
Cause/reason linking: 風邪をひいて学校を休んだ (Caught a cold and missed school)
Ongoing state with いる: 本を読んでいる (Is reading a book)
Permission with もいい: 帰ってもいいですか (May I go home?)
Prohibition with はいけない: ここで走ってはいけない (You must not run here)
cautions: Exception: 行く becomes 行って (not 行いて) — the only godan く-verb with this irregular て-form
The て-form itself carries no tense — the tense comes from the final verb in the sentence
When linking sequential actions, the て-form implies the actions happen in order — reversing the order changes the meaning
続けて (from 続ける) uses て-form as 'means': 5週続けて増えている = has been increasing for 5 weeks running (continuing by means of consecutive weeks)

summary: いく marks movement away from the speaker's location or away from the speaker's empathy point. Like くる, this perspectival meaning applies whether いく stands alone or follows a conjunctive て-form verb. In narrative, いく marks departure away from the story's focus point.
subUses: Motion away from speaker: 友達が行った (My friend left)
Narrative departure from focus: やっとあんしんしてかえっていきました (finally, relieved, they went home [away from the stump scene])
Carrying something away: かれくさをもっていく (take dried grass away)
cautions: うちにいく is ungrammatical for going to one's own home — use うちにかえる
When the speaker identifies psychologically with the destination, くる replaces いく even across phone calls or in writing — if unsure, ask whether the speaker's empathy is at the destination

summary: くる marks movement toward the speaker's location or toward wherever the speaker has placed their empathy or viewpoint — which need not be the speaker's physical position. This perspectival meaning applies whether くる stands alone or follows a conjunctive て-form verb (the preceding verb describes manner; くる carries the directionality). In narrative, くる marks movement toward the story's empathy center (often a key place or protagonist).
subUses: Motion toward speaker's location: 友達が来た (My friend came [here])
Visitor adopts host's location as reference: 明日来てもいいですか (May I come tomorrow? — said by the visitor)
Narrative empathy center: のねずみがかれくさをもってきて、きりかぶにかぶせました (the field mouse brought dried grass and covered the stump [stump is the story's focus point])
Speaker's "territory" includes workplace: 明日、会社に来てください (Please come to my office tomorrow)
cautions: The くる/いく choice depends on whose viewpoint the speaker adopts, not physical proximity: use くる when the speaker's empathy is at the destination (movement is toward the speaker's viewpoint); use いく when the speaker's empathy stays at the origin (movement is away from the speaker's viewpoint)

summary: Potential verbs express ability — 'can do' or 'be able to do.' For godan verbs, change the final う-row to the え-row + る (書く→書ける, 話す→話せる); for ichidan verbs, drop る and add られる (食べる→食べられる); irregulars: する→できる, くる→こられる. Potential verbs themselves conjugate as ichidan verbs.
subUses: Expressing ability: 日本語が読める (I can read Japanese)
Expressing possibility due to circumstances: 今日は早く帰れる (I can go home early today)
Negative inability: 辛いものが食べられない (I can't eat spicy food)
cautions: ら抜き言葉: 食べれる/見れる (dropping ら from ichidan potential) are widely used in casual speech — do not mark wrong, but note it is colloquial
The particle often shifts from を to が with potential verbs: ピアノが弾ける (can play piano) — but を is also acceptable in modern Japanese
For ichidan verbs, potential られる is identical in form to passive られる — context distinguishes them
できる (potential of する) is a standalone verb, not a conjugation pattern — it takes が for the object: 料理ができる (can cook)

Do not use させられる in quiz sentences for this topic — させられる is causative-passive ('being forced to do'), which is a confusing form that belongs to a different grammar topic. Stick to clear potential forms like 書ける, 食べられる, できる

summary: ぬ and つ are Classical Japanese completion auxiliaries that mark an action as finished or perfected (完了), roughly corresponding to modern た or てしまう. They attach to the 連用形 (continuative stem) of verbs. The two differ by verb type: ぬ marks natural completion or the completion of intransitive/non-volitional events, while つ marks volitional, deliberate completion, typically with transitive verbs.
subUses: Natural/non-volitional completion (ぬ): 春立ちぬ (spring has arrived — natural occurrence)
Deliberate/volitional completion (つ): 文書きつ (I have finished writing the letter — intentional act)
Terminal ぬ as plain completion: 散りぬ (has scattered — ぬ in its 終止形 (しゅうしけい, plain sentence-final form), marking completed natural action)
Attributive form (ぬる / つる) modifying a noun: 散りぬる花 (flowers that have fallen) / かねつる涙 (tears that could not be stopped)
cautions: Completion ぬ (連体形 ぬる) is a distinct auxiliary from negation ぬ (the 連体形 of ず, meaning 'does not'): both look like ぬ in the 終止形 but conjugate differently and mean opposite things

つ (completion) is not the same as the modern te-form て; in Classical Japanese つ is a standalone auxiliary with its own full paradigm (終止形 (しゅうしけい, plain sentence-final form) つ, 連体形 つる, 已然形 (いぜんけい, realis form — the 'already-thus' form, used after こそ and in 'since/because' conditionals) つれ)
ぬ and つ appear stacked together (ぬつ / つぬ) in Classical poetry for rhetorical alternation — this is not a mistake or redundancy
The rule that ぬ = intransitive and つ = transitive is a useful tendency, not an absolute law; poetic usage sometimes reverses them

summary: Bound particles (係助詞) are Classical Japanese particles that impose a grammatical constraint on the predicate at the end of their clause: the final verb must appear in a specific inflected form rather than the plain 終止形 (しゅうしけい, plain sentence-final form). Most bound particles (ぞ、なむ、や、か) require the 連体形 (attributive form); こそ alone requires the 已然形 (realis form). The binding relationship is called 係り結び (かかりむすび, kakari-musubi — the binding relationship between the bound particle and the sentence-final verb form). The particles themselves carry emphatic, interrogative, or emotional nuances.
subUses: ぞ — strong emphasis or assertive focus, requires 連体形: 止めそかねつる (could not stop; ぞ forces the final verb into 連体形 つる)
こそ — strong emphasis with 已然形 binding, often concessive: 命こそ惜しけれ (it is life that is dear to me)
や / か — rhetorical question or doubt, require 連体形: これや都鳥 (is this the bird of the capital?)
なむ — softer emphasis (modern なん), requires 連体形: 男もすなる日記 (a diary that men also write, so they say)
cautions: In modern Japanese は and も survive from the bound-particle system but have lost their binding requirement; do not expect modern は to force 連体形
こそ…已然形 is the sole exception to the 連体形 rule: every other bound particle triggers 連体形, but こそ triggers 已然形 — mixing them up is a common Classical Japanese reading error
係り結び is a Classical phenomenon; it does not apply to modern Japanese predicates even when classical-style particles like ぞ appear in song lyrics or quotations

summary: ず is the classical negation auxiliary, equivalent to modern ない. It attaches to the 未然形 (irrealis stem) of verbs: 恐れず (without fearing), 見ず (not seeing), せず (not doing). The IMABI entry focuses on its classical conjugation and literary uses. In modern Japanese, ず survives primarily as an adverbial/conjunctive form meaning 'without doing X' (何も恐れず進む = advance without fearing anything) and in set phrases.
subUses: Adverbial/conjunctive negation (ず used mid-sentence): 何も恐れず進むの (advancing without fearing anything)
Literary attributive negation (ぬ, the 連体形 of ず): 帰らぬ人 (a person who does not return)
Set phrase ざるを得ない (cannot avoid doing): 認めざるを得ない (have no choice but to admit)
Set phrase ずにはいられない (cannot help doing): 笑わずにはいられない (can't help laughing)
cautions: This topic covers the classical auxiliary ず and its direct survivals in modern literary/set-phrase usage. The closely related modern grammar points ずに (without doing, N3), ずにはいられない, and ざるを得ない are each separate grammar topics — do not conflate them here
する negates to せず (not しず or すず); くる negates to こず
The 連体形 of ず is ぬ (e.g. 知らぬ), which looks identical to the completion auxiliary ぬ — context (verb transitivity, whether completion or negation is meant) distinguishes them

summary: む is a classical Japanese auxiliary (推量助動詞, すいりょうじょどうし, conjectural auxiliary) that attaches to the irrealis / 未然形 stem of a verb. It expresses either conjecture about an outcome — parallel to modern だろう — or a volitional invitation encouraging action — parallel to the modern volitional ending (〜よう). Because む covers both meanings, context determines which reading applies.
subUses: Conjecture (like modern だろう): またいつか会わむ (I suppose we shall meet again someday)
Volitional or hortative (like modern 〜よう): 帰りみむ (let us look back [on the road home])
cautions: む attaches to the 未然形 (irrealis stem), the same stem used with ない — not the dictionary form
The two readings (conjecture vs. volitional) depend entirely on context; do not mark either interpretation wrong without evidence from surrounding text
む is classical and appears mainly in poetry, classical literature, and traditional song — do not expect it in modern prose or conversation

summary: き is the archaic attributive (連体形) form of い-adjectives. In modern Japanese the attributive form ends in い and is identical to the dictionary form, but in classical and literary Japanese the final い was き (e.g. 遠い → 遠き). The transition from き to い is a historical sound change called イ音便 (いおんびん, i-sound euphony — the shift of き → い in adjective endings). Today き survives in literary, poetic, or highly formal writing and in a few fixed expressions.
subUses: Modifying a following noun in literary or poetic style: 遠き昔の話 (a tale of the distant past)
Fixed or set expressions that have not shifted to modern い: 愛しき人 (beloved person)
Archaic speech style in traditional song or poetry: 夢は遠きまぼろしに (dreams fade into far-off phantoms)
cautions: き is an archaic replacement for modern い in the adjective's noun-modifying position only — it does not appear in predicate position (do not accept 空は青き as a complete modern sentence)
Do not confuse with the noun き (tree/spirit) or the verb stem き from くる — the presence of an adjective stem before き makes it unambiguous
